coredata.dc:title: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots using chitosan and 4-hydroxycoumarin as the precursors for identification of Al3+ and tetracyclines and its applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yong-qing
Liang, Le
Huang, Chan
Wang, Ye
Han, Mei
Hu, Qi-fan
Liu, Yu-lan
Cai, Xiao-hua
coredata.dc:description: Aluminum and tetracyclines (TCs) are important environmental pollutants, and the development of simple and efficient analytic techniques is of great significance. In this work, an economical nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from chitosan and 4-hydroxycoumarin was prepared through one-step hydrothermal process. The N-CDs with blue fluorescence emission exhibited satisfactory water solubility, stability and biocompatibility. When the addition of Al3+ or TCs, the N-CDs solution presents obvious fluorescence enhancing or quenching, Al3+ can make the fluorescence in the N-CDs-TCs system recover. The N-CDs sensor platform shows satisfactory selectivity and sensitivity in detection of Al3+, tetracycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline, and their low detection limits are 0.857 μM, 120 μM, 127 μM, and 117 μM, respectively. The N-CDs sensor can be applied for the detection of trace Al3+ and TCs in real samples such as tap water and milk, moreover, possible mechanisms on the combination of N-CDs with Al3+ and TCs also were investigated by IR, UV, XPS analysis and DFT calculations. In addition, the N-CDs with good water solubility and biocompatibility can be used to observe the hatching process for medaka fish embryo and fluorescence bio-imaging to indicate that it may have good application potential in biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
N-CDs
Al3+ detection
Tetracyclines detection
Fluorescence analysis
Bio-imaging

Detection of Al3+ and TCs test
Selectivity and sensitivity for N-CDs to Al3+ detection
Mechanism for N-CDs to Al3+ detection
Fluorescence response of N-CDs-TCH to Al3+
N-CDs to TCs and Al3+detection in actual samples
Fluorescence imaging in medaka fish embryo culture

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the N-CDs (Inset are HRTEM image and size distribution histograms); (b) Fourier transform infrared spectra of N-CDs, CTS and 4-hydroxycoumarin; (c) Raman spectrum of the N-CDs; (d) XRD pattern of the N-CDs.
(a) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CDs; (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s; (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s; (d) High-resolution XPS spectra of O1s.

(a) The UV absorption spectrum of N-CDs (black line), fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum (inset photo of N-CDs solution under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right); (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs were obtained at different excitation wavelengths (from 300 nm to 410 nm, with an interval of 10 nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra and photos of N-CDs (under λ = 365 nm ultraviolet lamp) after the addition of metal ions; (b) Ultraviolet absorption spectra of N-CDs and after the addition of metal ions; (c) Fluorescence anti-interference tests for N-CDs to Al3+ detection; (d) Ultraviolet absorption anti-interference tests for N-CDs to Al3+ detection.

(a) The fluorescence spectra for Al3+ to N-CDs titration range from 1.0–45 μM and the corresponding photos; (b) The nonlinear fluorescence curve for Al3+ to N-CDs titration from 1.0–45 μM and linear equation Al3+ to N-CDs titration (1.0–10.0 μM).
(a) Full-scan XPS spectrum of N-CDs-Al3+; (b) XPS spectrum of 2p of Al; (c) XPS spectrum: N1s of N-CDs-Al3+; (d) XPS spectrum: O1s of N-CDs-Al3+.
Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (a, c, e) under different concentrations of TCH, OTC and CTC; (b, d, f) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity with TCH, OTC and CTC concentration at 420 nm.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry curve measured in N-CDs solution; (b) Optical band gap calculated by UV spectrum of N-CDs; (c) Schematic diagram of electron transfer between N-CDs and TCH; (d) UV absorption TCH (black line) and excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs.

(a) N-CDs-TCH fluorescence recovery under different concentrations of Al3+; (b) linear relationship between Al3+ concentration and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-TCH at 472 nm; (c) Fluorescence spectra for N-CDs-TCH system with different metal ions; (d) UV absorption spectra of the combination of N-CDs, N-CDs-TCH, N-CDs-TCH with Al3+.
Fluorescence imaging N-CDs was used in the incubation process of medaka fish embryo.
The preparation and applications of N-CDs.
Possible mechanism on the combination of N-CDs with Al3+.
The quenching mechanism diagram of TCs toward N-CDs.

head.ce:title: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots using chitosan and 4-hydroxycoumarin as the precursors for identification of Al3+ and tetracyclines and its applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Minzu University, Guiyang 550025 PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Minzu University, Guiyang 550025, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aluminum and tetracyclines (TCs) are important environmental pollutants, and the development of simple and efficient analytic techniques is of great significance. In this work, an economical nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from chitosan and 4-hydroxycoumarin was prepared through one-step hydrothermal process. The N-CDs with blue fluorescence emission exhibited satisfactory water solubility, stability and biocompatibility. When the addition of Al3+ or TCs, the N-CDs solution presents obvious fluorescence enhancing or quenching, Al3+ can make the fluorescence in the N-CDs-TCs system recover. The N-CDs sensor platform shows satisfactory selectivity and sensitivity in detection of Al3+, tetracycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline, and their low detection limits are 0.857 μM, 120 μM, 127 μM, and 117 μM, respectively. The N-CDs sensor can be applied for the detection of trace Al3+ and TCs in real samples such as tap water and milk, moreover, possible mechanisms on the combination of N-CDs with Al3+ and TCs also were investigated by IR, UV, XPS analysis and DFT calculations. In addition, the N-CDs with good water solubility and biocompatibility can be used to observe the hatching process for medaka fish embryo and fluorescence bio-imaging to indicate that it may have good application potential in biomedical field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aluminum as one of the most prevalent metal element has extensive use in daily life and industrial fields [1]. But excessive intake of aluminum can harm the central nervous system to result in Alzheimer’s disease, memory loss and osteoporosis [2–5]. In addition, Al3+ is a major factor to cause plant production limiting due to its good water-solubility and easily absorbed by plants [6]. At present, there were a large number of methods for quantitative analysis of Al3+, such as atomic absorption spectrum, electrochemical analysis, high-performance liquid chromatography, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and voltammetry, etc. [7–10]. However, these technologies usually require expensive instrumentation, complex operations, and long analytical time to limit their applications in a way. By contrast, fluorescence analysis shows more advantages including low-cost, rapid response, convenient operation, superior selectivity and sensitivity.

With the massive use of antibiotics, the detection of its residues has become an important task in modern society. Tetracyclines (TCs), as a type of broad-spectrum antibiotics, were used to treat infectious diseases from humans and animals in the past decades [11,12]. Now TCs are less used in the treatment of human diseases in view of their adverse effects, but broadly act as the drugs to treat infectious diseases and promote animal growth in animal husbandry [13]. The overuse of TCs can cause the accumulation of antibiotic residues in drinking water and animal products to load to bacteria resistance, which brings serious threat to human health [14]. Therefore, it is very crucial for accurately detection of TCs content in livestock products, environmental, and water bodies. Conventional detection methods for TCs include immunoassay (IA) [15], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16,17], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [18], and electrochemical method (ECR) [19,20], etc. However, these analytic techniques usually required expensive instruments, complicated operation, time-consuming procedures, and skilled operator. Hence, it is of great significance to develop a simple, economical and efficient system for rapidly determination of TCs. Fluorescent sensors with many advantages such as simple operation, rapid response, and more sensitivity and selectivity provide better choices for ions and organic small detection [21–23].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial with the size of less than 10 nm, has attracted widespread attention due to the superior optical properties, low-toxicity, high photostability and satisfactory biocompatibility [24,25]. CDs has widely been used in many areas including fluorescent sensor, finger-print recognition, biomedicine, photocatalysis, food safety, etc [26–30]. The uses of carbon dots for detection of Al3+ [31–38] and TCs [39–47] had been reported in the past literature, but the CDs for bifunctional monitoring Al3+ and TCs are fewer. Shuang and coworkers successively developed the CDs sensing platform for detection of tetracyclines (TCs) and Al3+. The CDs were directly quenched by TCs, which can partly be recovered by Al3+ through a fluorescence “on–off–on” pathway [48,49]. Despite the above achievements, fewer CDs derived from bio-materials for the detection of TCs and Al3+ were found, moreover, lack of mechanistic studies in-depth up to now. Thus it still is necessary to develop simple and efficient CDs for detecting Al3+ and TCs with highly selectivity and sensitivity from economical biological sources.

CDs based on biomass sources have always attracted much attention due to their low-cost, satisfactory security and biocompatibility in recent years. Chitosan as a kind of natural biological macromolecule derived from the deacetylation of chitin widely exists in nature [50,51]. The natural polymer occupies a large number of amino (–NH2) and hydroxyl (–OH) functional groups and shows excellent properties of biological function, blood compatibility, safety and microbial degradation. 4-Hydroxy coumarin is an intermediate of pharmaceutical and pesticide, and its hydroxyl group can be substituted the amine group from chitosan to cause significant fluorescence enhancement. In this paper, a simple and low-cost nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with blue fluorescent emission derived from lactose and 4-hydroxy coumarin was prepared by one-step hydrothermal process. The N-CDs exhibits unique optical performances and can conveniently identify and detect Al3+ and TCs with rapid time-response, higher selectivity and sensitivity. Interestingly, Al3+ can efficiently enhance the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs, but the addition of TCs directly lead to the fluorescence quenching of the system. In addition, the possible combination and fluorescence change mechanism between CDs with Al3+ or TCs and its potential applications are also investigated.

Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. supplies chitosan and 4-hydroxycoumarin. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC) and chloramphenicol hydrochloride (CTC) were purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. The metal ions, the other antibiotics, amino acids and small molecules were from Beijing Chemical Reagent Factory. All chemical reagents used in experiments were analytic degree of purity and without any purified. Ultrapure water was used to prepare various testing solutions. The surface morphology was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Yonke Optical Instruments Co., LTD.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co, USA) was used to confirm the surface chemical state of the material. The fluorescence spectra were obtained from the fluorescence spectrophotometer of Baihe Instrument Technology Co., LTD. (F-7000, Shanghai, China). FTIR-650S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tianjin Port East) collects the FT-IR spectra. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were carried out at ATU-1810DPC spectrometer (Tianjin Gangdong). Electrochemical analyzer SC28-CHI660E (Shanghai Advantage Chemical Co., LTD.) was used to measure zeta potential. Inverted fluorescence microscope (WMF-3680) from Shanghai Yuguang Instrument Co., LTD provided bio-imaging.

N-CDs was synthesized through a simple hydrothermal process using chitosan and 4-hydroxycoumarin as the precursors. Firstly, chitosan (0.5 g), 4-hydroxy-coumarin (1 g) and 10 mL deionized water were added into a polytetrafluoroethylene tank, and 5 mL of hydrochloric acid were introduced to the solution to make the pH value reach 1.0. Then the mixture was placed in a high-temperature reactor and heated for 8 h at 180 °C. The solution was cooled to room temperature, and the yellow liquid was filtered through a needle cartridge filter with a pore size of 0.22 μ. Finally, the N-CDs solution was refrigerated at 4 °C for future use. The powder of N-CDs was obtained through drying collected supernatant in the oven at 50 °C. The diagram for the synthetic process and optical properties of N-CDs were shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of fluorescence quantum yields of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative quantum yield (QY ) of N-CDs was measured using quinine sulfate (dispersed in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54 %) as a reference, and the resulting quantum yield of N-CDs were calculated to be 23 %. The calculated Eq. (1) as follows:
(1)
Q Y
=
Q R
I /
I R
×
O
D R
/
OD
×
n 2
/
n R 2
QY and QR respectively represent the fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs and quinine sulfate (φ = 0.546, refractive index n = 1.33), I and IR are the measured integrated FL intensity of NCDs and quinine sulfate. OD and ODR respectively stand for absorbance density of N-CDs and quinine sulfate (often less than 0.05), n and nR is the refractive index of N-CDs and quinine sulfate. All the data of the solution were obtained under the same specification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ and TCs test
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared N-CDs solution as a fluorescent probe was used to detect Al3+ and TCs. By adding various metal ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mn2+), antibiotics (erythromycin, chloramphenicol, kanamycin, norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, amoxicillin, chloramphenicol, Ibuprofen) and amino acids (lysine, aspartate, glutathione, glutamic acid, cysteine) to investigate the selectivity for N-CDs to Al3+ and TCs detection, respectively. N-CDs was diluted to 50 times with deionized water, 500 μL of metal ions, antibiotics and amino acids solution (100 μM) were respectively added to N-CDs solution, then diluted to 3 mL, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity at 352 nm was recorded. The excitation and emission slit width was set to 10 nm with a gain of 1.0 nm. In addition, the concentration dependence for N-CDs to Al3+ and TCs determination was also inspected. The fluorescence intensity at emission (410 nm) and excitation wavelength (352 nm) was studied by the addition of different amounts of Al3+ and TCs into 3 mL of diluted N-CDs solution.

The N-CDs aqueous solution is a yellow solution that displays stronger blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. There are a large number of functional groups such as OH, CO, OCO and NH on the surface of N-CDs. The fluorescence of N-CDs solution enhances when the addition of Al3+, but TCs can lead to its fluorescence quenching. Interestingly, Al3+ can recover the fluorescence of the mixed solution of N-CDs and TCs.

The morphology, particle size and dispersion of N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It can observe from Fig. 1 a that most of N-CDs are spherical, evenly distributed and dispersed. After further magnification, the lattice fringes can be clearly seen in the TEM image, and the average lattice spacing of each nanoparticle was measured to be about 0.176 nm, which is similar to the spatial structure of the graphitic carbon lattice. The distribution range is about 1∼5.5 nm, the average particle size is about 3.2 nm, and the particle size distribution is a Gaussian fitting linear curve.

The functional groups and chemical composition of N-CDs can be analyzed through FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b). Since chitosan (CTS) itself has a large number of OH and NH2 functional groups, the characteristic absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of OH and NH [52]. The stretching vibration at 2956 cm−1 is CH, and the characteristic peaks of primary amino NH and CN respectively appear at 1572 cm−1 and 1441 cm−1 [53]. The absorption peaks of CO and CO are respectively located at 1630 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 [54]. For 4-hydroxycoumarin (4-HC), the OH stretching vibration is obvious at 3450 cm−1, and the peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration of the CO and CO bonds, respectively. Compared to chitosan, the OH or NH stretching vibrations of N-CDs present an apparent decrease at 3430 cm−1, and the vibrations of CH at 1130 cm−1 related to the pyranose almost disappeared, indicating the degradation of the chitosan chain and decomposition of the pyranose ring through dehydration [53]. In addition, the vibration of the CN bond can be observed at 1441 cm−1, which proves that nitrogen is successfully doped into N-CDs. Raman spectrum of N-CDs shows two typical peaks at 1348 cm−1 (D-band) and 1598 cm−1 (G-band), respectively representing Csp3 with disordered carbon structure and Csp2 with lattice structure (Fig. 1c) [55]. The intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) is 0.84 (<1), which further indicates the high graphitization configuration of N-CDs [56]. The XRD diffraction pattern Fig. 1d shows a broad peak at 23.6°, suggesting that N-CDs exhibit a disordered pattern during the introduction of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups [57–59].

XPS can be used to characterize the structure and composition of N-CDs. There are three characteristic peaks in XPS graph, which are located at 258.12 eV, 399.99 eV and 532.43 eV, and indicating that N-CDs mainly contains carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, accounting for 68.47 %, 5.93 % and 25.6 % respectively (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution spectrum C1s is separated into 288.4 eV, 286.3 eV, 285.2 eV three peaks, belonging to CO, CO/CN, CC/CC (Fig. 2b) [60]. The C–Csp2 characteristic peak at 285.2 eV indicates the prepared N-CDs with rich graphite structure. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s characterizes the absorption peaks at NH2 (399.2 eV), CN (400.1 eV), and NH (401.9 eV) (Fig. 2c). The CO (530.6 eV), COH (532.7 eV) and OCO groups (533 eV) present in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d). These results from XPS further confirm that N atoms have been successfully doped into CDs, and many rich functional groups have been formed on the CDs surface, such as CN, COH, CO, OCO and NH.

The optical properties of N-CDs were characterized by ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy, The absorption peak observed at 255 nm was caused by the π–π* transition (CC bond) of the graphitic carbon nuclei in N-CDs [61]. There also is an absorption peak between 300 nm and 400 nm, which may be attributed to the n–π* transition of the CO bond [62]. As shown in (Fig. 3 a), these absorption peaks may be due to the abundant functional groups of N-CDs doped by nitrogen. The fluorescent changes of N-CDs in excitation wavelength attributes to different size particles and emission traps on their surface [63]. N-CDs displays blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Scheme 1), when the fluorescence was captured at 352 nm wavelength, the best fluorescence emission of N-CDs appeared at 420 nm wavelength. Obviously, the emission wavelength of N-CDs is closely related to the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 300 nm to 410 nm, the maximum emission wavelength moves from 420 nm to 510 nm (Fig. 3b). The redshift may be determined by the distribution of different sizes and multi-surface emission sites of N-CDs [64]. The studies on N-CDs can also confirmed that the optical properties may correspond to fluorescent surface groups [65]. In addition, it is worth mentioning that the N-CDs exhibits excellent water-solubility and stability at room temperature, no floating or precipitated nanoparticles appeared even after one month of storage in the air. Moreover, there was no significant discoloration and fluorescence fading after 2 h of irradiation under a 365 nm UV lamp, which indicates that the N-CDs may be a fluorescent material with good application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity for N-CDs to Al3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: As a fluorescent sensor, the selectivity and anti-interference are its important functions. To illustrate the selectivity of N-CDs for detection of Al3+, 500 μL of different metal ions (100 μM) were added to 3 mL of N-CDs solution to investigate their response. It could observe that there were no changes in fluorescence intensity of N-CDs when the addition of Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, but the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs significantly enhances when Al3+ was added to N-CDs solution, and followed a wavelength red-shift (Fig. 4 a). These results demonstrated that N-CDs as a sensor for Al3+ detection had significant selectivity. As shown in Fig. 4b, the ultraviolet absorption spectra of N-CDs and N-CD + Mn+ solution were similar, but a new absorption peak appeared at 291 nm, and the original absorption peak at 328 nm disappears when 100 μM of Al3+ is added to the solution of N-CD. The results may due to the combination of functional groups on the surface of N-CDs with Al3+ to form a complex, but the other metal ions have little effect on the ultraviolet absorption of N-CDs. Subsequently, the anti-interference experiments of the ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence (FL) of N-CDs-Al3+ were carried out as shown in Fig. 4c–d. The other coexisting ions had no effects on ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence emission of N-CDs, indicating that N-CDs had good selectivity and anti-jamming capabilities.

To evaluate the sensitivity for N-CDs to Al3+ detection, different concentrations of Al3+ were added to N-CDs solutions to carry out the fluorescence titration experiment. As shown in Fig. 5 a, with the concentration of Al3+ from 1.0 μM to 45 μM, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 470 nm gradually increased and the corresponding color also became brighter under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. There was a good linear relationship between fluorescence enhancement and Al3+ concentration in the range from1.0–10.0 μM, and the linear fitting equation and correlation coefficients were respectively Y = 32.296X + 526.1160 and R2 = 0.9967. Based on the acquainted equation “LOD = 3σ/k” (S/N = 3) [66], the detection limit was estimated to be 0.857 μM, which demonstrated that the N-CDs could act as a sensor to sensitively detect Al3+ in complex environment (Fig. 5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for N-CDs to Al3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: As previously reported in the literature, most of N-CDs are sensitive to metal ions due to the abundance of amino and hydroxyl groups in their surface, which are conducive to the coordination with Al3+ to form complexes and change the optical properties of N-CDs. The functional groups on the surface of N-CDs such as NH, CO can combine with electron-deficient Al3+ to result in the d-orbital splitting of Al3+ to participating in new hybridization. Therefore, part electrons of the N-CDs in the excited state are transferred to d-orbital of Al3+, the electron transition can enhance the fluorescence emission.

In order to further verify the mechanism for fluorescence enhancement, the N-CDs-Al3+ complex was characterized by XPS. After the addition of Al3+, the XPS of full scan of N-CDs (Fig. 6 a) and the characteristic peak of Al2p spectrum at 75 eV (Fig. 6b) indicated the presence of Al. In N 1s/Al3+, the original NH2 absorption peak at 399.2 eV disappeared, indicating that the functional group (NH2) of chitosan reacted with the hydroxyl from 4-hydroxycoumarin (4-HC) to remove a molecule of water and form an amino group (NH) which can act with Al3+. A new absorption peak appears at 407.5 eV, which may be a new N-Al3+ bond after the coordination of NH with Al3+ (Fig. 6c). In O 1s/Al3+, the characteristic peak of the OCO bond obviously becomes weaker, and it is possible that the OCO bond coordinates with Al3+ (Fig. 6d). For the above conjecture description, a schematic diagram for fluorescence enhancement is described as shown in Scheme 2 .

The N-CDs can also serve as a chemical sensor for TCs detection. To further demonstrate the selectivity of N-CDs to TCs detection, 500 μL of various antibiotics (erythromycin, chloramphenicol, kanamycin, norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, amoxicillin, ibuprofen) and amino acids (lysine, aspartic acid, glutathione, glutamic acid, glutamic acid) were respectively added to 3 mL of N-CDs solution. 100 μM concentration of various antibiotic or amino acid were fixed to record the fluorescence response of N-CDs. It could observe from Fig. S1 that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs had no obvious change when the addition of above antibiotics and amino acids, but TCs could cause obvious fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. In order to explore the selectivity of N-CDs-TCs, anti-interference tests were performed when N-CDs co-existed with other antibiotics and amino acid molecules (Fig. S2), the fluorescence quenching effect was stable and had no obvious change trend, indicating that N-CDs had special recognition ability for TCs and could use to detect TCs in complex environment.

It could observe that the initial fluorescence of N-CDs quenched by TCs. With the increase of TCs concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs slowly decreased and presented a good linear relationship with TCs concentration when the addition 0–50 μM and 50–120 μM of TCH. The corresponding linear fitting equations were Y = −8.762X + 752.254 (R2 = 0.982) and Y = −3.396X + 495.788 (R2 = 0.992), respectively. According to the acquainted equation “LOD = 3σ/k” (S/N = 3) [66], the lowest detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 120 μM (Fig. 7 a and b). Likewise, for OTC, when the concentration of OTC was 0–60 μM and 60–140 μM, the corresponding linear fitting equations were Y = −6.678X + 747.298 (R2 = 0.982) and Y = −3.105X + 554.858 (R2 = 0.984), respectively, and the lowest detection limit (LOD) was 127 μM (Fig. 7c and d). When the concentration of CTC was 0–120 μM, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with CTC concentration, and the linear fitting equation was Y = −5.486X + 768.375 (R2 = 0.996), and the lowest detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 117 μM (Fig. 7e and f). It is worth mentioning that the quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 of TCH, OTC and CTC toward N-CDs are 87 %, 85 % and 84 % respectively, and they were almost the same might attribute to their similar molecular structure of TCs. There are phenolic hydroxyl and carbonylgroups in TCs which can easily combine with ester carbonyl(OCO) and amino (NH) groups on the surface of N-CDs by hydrogen bonds to cause the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. The speculated quenching mechanism on for TCs to N-CDs was shown as depicted in Scheme 3 .

TCH as a representing TCs is used for further studies because of its relatively high quenching efficiency. To further confirm whether there is electron transfer in the process of N-CDs quenched by TCH, the highest occupied molecular orbital energy level (HOMO) and lowest occupied orbital energy level (LUMO) of N-CDs can be verified according to the formula (2) and (3):
(2)
E
H O M O
= - e
E OX
+ 4.4
(3)
E
LUMO
= - e
E
red
+ 4.4

The cyclic voltammetry curve of N-CDs in solution is determined, where EOX and Ered respectively represents the oxidation and reduction potential of N-CDs. According to formula (2) and (3), Ered and ELUMO can be calculated to be −0.213 eV and −4.613 eV (Fig. 8 a). Since the oxidation process is irreversible, the light energy band gap of N-CDs can be calculated by continuing the ultraviolet absorption spectrum, and the tangent site is the desired light energy band gap Eg = 5.56 eV (Fig. 8b). The energy level is calculated by combining Eg and Ered as shown in formula (4) [67], therefore, the EHUMO value is calculated to be −10.173 eV.
(4)
E
H O MO
=
E LUMO
-
E g

As previously reported in B3LYP in Dmol mode, the EHOMO and ELUMO of TCH can be calculated to respectively be −6.2 eV and −3.87 eV in view of the LUMO orbital energy level of TCH is higher than that of the LUMO orbital of N-CDs, the photoexcited electrons of N-CDs cannot be transferred to the LUMO of TCH [68]. According to energy level calculations, it can be known that no photoinduced electron transfer (PET) can occur between N-CDs and TCH (Fig. 8c) [69]. In order to prove that the detection mechanism of TCs is IFE, the infrared spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs-TCH are compared (Fig. S3). There are no significant changes in the absorption peak of N-CDs-TCH and N-CDs, and no obvious red and blue shifts or new characteristic absorption peaks can be observed, indicating that no new functional groups are generated. In addition, the excitation spectrum of N-CDs can partially overlap with the ultraviolet absorption band of TCH, suggesting that the internal filtration effect (IFE) also contributes to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 8d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of N-CDs-TCH to Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution quenched by TCH can gradually be recovered by Al3+. When Al3+ concentration range from 0–110 μM, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution gradually recovered, and the maximum fluorescence recovery degree of N-CDs-TCH system reached 82 %, which presented a good linear relationship with Al3+concentration (Fig. 9 a). The corresponding linear fitting equation and the correlation coefficient respectively were Y = 4.181X + 205.401 and R2 = 0.982, and the lowest detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 102 μM (Fig. 9b). In order to explore the selectivity of the N-CDs-TCH system to Al3+, 500 μL of different metal ions (c = 100 μM) were added to the N-CDs-TCH system, only the N-CDs-TCH system with Al3+ exhibited prominent fluorescent changes (Fig. 9c). Subsequently, the ultraviolet absorption of the N-CDs-TCH system was investigated after the addition of Al3+. Compared with the N-CDs-TCH system, the absorption band at 355 nm disappeared, and a new peak at 375 nm appeared again like the previous N-CDs system ( Fig. 9d). Therefore, it can speculate that the affinity of Al3+ to N-CDs is stronger than that of TCH, so the addition of Al3+ causes TCH to escape from the surface of N-CDs because Al3+ chelates with N-CDs to form a stable complex to release the N-CDs with fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs to TCs and Al3+detection in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of N-CDs as a fluorescence sensor, the content of TCs in tap water and milk samples was determined. TCs (5, 10, 15, 20 μM) were dispersed into tap water and milk samples. As shown in Table S1, the TCH, OTC and CTC recoveries of tap water samples were 97.48–99.23 %, 96.62–101 % and 96.2–102.6 %, respectively. It can observe from Table S2 that The TCH, OTC and CTC recoveries of the samples in milk were 96.42–100.97 %, 96.7–105.08 % and 96.32–102.13 %, respectively, the relative deviations were less than 5 %. In addition, taking TCH representing as a TCs example, various concentrations of Al3+ (20, 40, 60, 80 μM) are added to the N-CDs-TCH system as shown in Table S3, the recoveries rate of Al3+ in tap water samples and milk samples respectively are 98.57–101.3 % and 96.55–99.72 %, and all the relative deviations were less than 4 %. The experimental results are satisfactory and verify the effectiveness of N-CDs a sensor for TCs and Al3+ detection in actual sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging in medaka fish embryo culture
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of N-CDs, N-CDs was diluted to 50 times as culture medium to incubate the embryo from medaka fish, and fluorescence microscope as a photographic tool was used to record the hatching process of the embryo. Fig. 10 a–b respectively present the fish embryo in aqueous solution under fluorescent and ultraviolet lamp, and Fig. 10c was the image of fish embryo in aqueous solution including N-CDs under ultraviolet lamp. From the above three sets of images, it can observe that N-CDs can clearly display fluorescence imaging of fish embryo. The incubation process of the embryo was filmed every two days, and it can find that the embryo can gradually take shape and develop into the appearance of small fish (Fig. 10d–f). After about a week of these embryo can successfully hatch to small fish, and Fig. 10g–i are the photos under UV light. These experiments confirmed that N-CDs could be used to observe the incubation process of the embryo through fluorescence imaging in organisms and exhibited good potential in the analysis of Al3+/TCs and bio-imaging fields.

In summary, an economical nitrogen-doped CDs with blue fluorescent emission were prepared by one-step hydrothermal process employing chitosan and 4-hydroxycoumarin (N-CDs) as the substrates. The N-CDs shows good water solubility, stability, biocompatibility and fluorescence emission with 23 % of relative quantum yield. Al3+ or TCs can make the N-CDs solution appear obvious fluorescence enhancing and quenching, and the addition of Al3+ can recover the fluorescence of the N-CDs-TCs solution. The N-CDs sensor platform exhibits satisfactory selectivity and sensitivity with the lowest detection limits to Al3+, tetracycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline are 0.857 μM, 120 μM, 127 μM, and 117 μM, respectively. The possible combination mechanisms on N-CDs with Al3+ and TCs to cause the changes of the N-CDs solution were also further discussed by IR, UV, XPS analysis and theoretical calculations. In addition, the sensor platform can be used to conveniently detect Al3+ and TCs in tap water and milk samples, especially, the N-CDs may be applied to observe the incubation process of the eggs through fluorescence imaging in organisms, and demonstrate it may have good potential in biomedical field.
All the authors consent to publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22104024) and the Natural Science Foundation of the Guizhou Science and Technology Department (ZK[2021]048).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125816.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
